coredata.dc:title: Highly photoluminescent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots as a green fluorescence probe for determination of myricetin
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Jingjing
Li, Peipei
Han, Yixiu
Li, Yuchen
Du, Yingxiang
coredata.dc:description: Highly photoluminescent N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs) which the quantum yield reached 63% were prepared through hydrothermal treatment. The obtained N-CDs displayed a uniform distribution of particle size, superior stability in high-salt conditions, and excellent sensitivity. A green fluorescence probe based on N-CDs was constructed for ultrasensitive determination of myricetin in vine tea on account of the static quenching. The N-CDs presented excellent linear fluorescence response in the concentration range of 0.2–40 μM and 56–112 μM and with a low detection limit of 56 nM. Additionally, the practicability of the probe was verified in spiked vine tea sample, and the satisfactory recoveries of myricetin varied from 98.8% to 101.2%, with relative standard deviations in the range of 1.52%-3.48%. It is the first time to employ N-CDs without any material modification as a fluorescence sensor to detect myricetin, which is a promising approach to expand the path for myricetin screening.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence probe
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Myricetin
Vine tea

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanism for the detection of myricetin
Sensitivity of N-CDs probe for myricetin detection
Analysis of myricetin in actual samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of N-CDs (Inset is HRTEM image). (B) The size distribution of N-CDs. (C) The FT-IR spectra of N-CDs.
XPS spectrum of N-CDs. (A) Full scan spectrum, (B) C1s spectrum, (C) N1s spectrum and (D) O1s spectrum.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of myricetin, excitation spectra, and emission spectra of the N-CDs. (B) Zeta potentials of N-CDs, myricetin and their mixtures under the same pH value (pH = 7). (C) Stern-Volmer plots under different temperatures. (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs, myricetin and their mixtures.
Suppressed efficiency of observed and corrected fluorescence of N-CQDs after addition of different concentrations of myricetin.

(A-B) Fluorescence response of N-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of myricetin. (C–D) The linear relationship of (F0-F)/F0 values to the concentration of myricetin over the range from 0.2 to 40 μM and 56 to 112 μM.

(A) The effect of NaCl concentration on the fluorescence intensity. The concentration of quantum dots is 0.96 μg/mL. (B) The stability of N-CDs in 21 days. (C) The specificity of the N-CDs for different structural analogues.(D) The effect of interfering ions (500 μM) and interfering substanceon(100 μM) the response of N-CDs when myricetin is present and absent (condition: pH 6.0, 0.1 M phosphate buffer, N-CDs: 0.96 μg/mL).
Schematic diagram about the preparation process of N-CDs and detection principle for myricetin.
Comparison of different quantification methods toward myricetin.
Spiked recoveries for myricetin estimated by the fluorescent probe (n = 3).

Initial
myricetin
amount
determined by
CDs(μM)

head.ce:title: Highly photoluminescent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots as a green fluorescence probe for determination of myricetin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Drug Quality Control and Pharmacovigilance (Ministry of Education), China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 210009, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Drug Quality Control and Pharmacovigilance (Ministry of Education)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Drug Quality Control and Pharmacovigilance (Ministry of Education), China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 210009, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Natural Medicines, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 210009, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Natural Medicines

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Natural Medicines, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 210009, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory of Drug Quality Control and Pharmacovigilance (Ministry of Education), China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 210009, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Drug Quality Control and Pharmacovigilance (Ministry of Education)

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Jingjing Chen and Peipei Li contribute equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly photoluminescent N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs) which the quantum yield reached 63% were prepared through hydrothermal treatment. The obtained N-CDs displayed a uniform distribution of particle size, superior stability in high-salt conditions, and excellent sensitivity. A green fluorescence probe based on N-CDs was constructed for ultrasensitive determination of myricetin in vine tea on account of the static quenching. The N-CDs presented excellent linear fluorescence response in the concentration range of 0.2–40 μM and 56–112 μM and with a low detection limit of 56 nM. Additionally, the practicability of the probe was verified in spiked vine tea sample, and the satisfactory recoveries of myricetin varied from 98.8% to 101.2%, with relative standard deviations in the range of 1.52%-3.48%. It is the first time to employ N-CDs without any material modification as a fluorescence sensor to detect myricetin, which is a promising approach to expand the path for myricetin screening.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Myricetin is a polyphenolic flavonoid compound which is widely distributed in berries, honey, vegetables, tea, and other daily plants (Tejero et al., 2007). Myricetin possesses extensively pharmacological activity, encompassing anti-oxidation, anti-inflammation, antiplatelet aggregation, anti-apoptosis, and hepatoprotective activity (Song et al., 2021, Deng et al., 2021). Furthermore, as active supplements in cosmetics, myricetin can prevent the generation of hydrogen peroxide to quench the photoaging free radicals in the skin (Wang et al., 2021). As food stabilizers and antimicrobial, the application of myricetin is popular in the food industry (Xing et al., 2019). As representatively bioactivities of tea, myricetin which was increased in the solid-state fermentation of the ripened Pu-erh has been confirmed to improve multiple health advantages such as modulating weight, regulating fatty acid metabolism (Sharma et al., 2021, Guo et al., 2021). Given the potential and extensive application of myricetin, the effective and sensitive quantification of myricetin in complicated samples is of great significance (Deng et al., 2021).

Actually, capillary electrophoresis (CE) (Şanlı and Lunte, 2014), electrochemical sensor (Xing et al., 2019, Huang et al.,2016), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Bi et al., 2013), high-performance liquid chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) (Sugahara et al., 2019, Sun et al., 2014, Becker Pertuzatti et al., 2021), and mass spectrometry (MS) (Kummer et al., 2015) had been acknowledged for the determination of myricetin. The aforementioned analytical methods are capable of tracking myricetin reliably and effectively. Nonetheless, the fluorescence sensor has gained tremendous attention due to its fast response and marvellous sensitivity. For example,Hu et al. designed a novel platform for the effective sensing of flavonoids by CdSe/ZnS quantum dots（Hu et al., 2014). Sadeghi et al. reported a simple turn on/off fluorescence approach based on dithizone-capped ZnS quantum dots (ZnS@DZ QDs).With the help of lead ions, the quantum dots can be used for quantitative detection of quercetin (Sadeghi and Hosseinpour-Zaryabi, 2020).

As an emerging fluorescent nanomaterial, carbon quantum dots (CDs) have been regarded as powerful candidates of organic dyes and conventional semiconductor quantum dots (Liu et al., 2020). Different precursors can supply different element doping and surface functional groups to tune the luminescent properties in the hydrothermal treatment (Wang et al., 2021). N-doping carbon quantum dots (N-CDs) possess outstanding properties, including excellent water dispersibility, fabrication simplicity, tunable photoluminescence, and strong photoluminescence (Deng et al., 2021, Wang et al., 2019, Zhang et al., 2020). Ascribed to the desirable properties, N-CDs was extensively applied in photo-catalysis, imaging, biological sensing (Dai et al., 2014, Bourzac, 2013, McHugh et al., 2018). Amin et al. reported one-pot hydrothermal carbonization route to prepared nitrogen-doped carbon dots using citric acid and l-lysine. The nanodot-based fluorescent sensors have been used for the quantitative detection of selenite (Amin et al., 2018).

In addition, the N-CDs-based fluorescence sensor also complies with the requirements of sustainable development and green chemistry standards. Kalanidhi et al. explore a green, cost effective route for preparation of carbon dots from betel leaves and ammonia as a green nitrogen dopant by hydrothermal method.The obtained fluorescent probe has been effectively used to detect picric acid with LOD of 0.11 μM (Kalanidhi et al., 2021). Yu et al. presented a green approach to synthesize fluorescent N-CQDs through one-step hydrothermal strategy using osmanthus fragrans. The off–on fluorescentnanosensorbased N-CQDs have been applied to detect quercetin and Al3+ content in fluid samples (Yu et al., 2021).

However, there is little research that uses a fluorescence sensor as a detection approach for investigating myricetin content. Liu and coworkers successfully synthesized CQDs (QY = 17%) to detect myricetin within 15 min (Liu et al., 2020).

Wang et al. synthesized deep eutectic solvents(DESs). And they obtained CDs with the assistance of DES to detect myricetin.The CDs (QY = 31.64%) were further surface modified using polyethyleneimine (Wang et al., 2021). Compared with above two methods, the proposed sensing system possessed wider linear range, shorter response time (within 1 min), easier synthetic routes. The N-CDs have higher quantum yields (QY = 63%) and better photo-stability.

Herein, as depicted in Scheme 1 , the prepared N-CDs-based fluorescence sensor which used diethylenetriamine and citric acid as precursors. The fluorescence intensity of the sensor decreased linearly with the increase of myricetin concentration. It was noteworthy that the N-CDs-based fluorescence sensor constructed a distinctive platform to track myricetin for the first time. Furthermore, the N-CDs-based fluorescence sensor is promising in practical applications for detecting myricetin.

Quinine sulfate, myricetin, coumarin, ethanol were purchased from Energy Chemical (Shanghai, China). Warfarin was obtained from J&K Scientific Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Ellagic acid, shikimic acid, Kaempferol, and diethylenetriamine all were purchased from Macklin Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Duloxetine hydrochloride, thalidomide all were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Propranolol hydrochloride was purchased from Meilun Biology Co., Ltd (Dalian, China). Copper sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4) and citric acid (CA) were supplied by Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Nanjing, China). Luteolin was purchased from Bidepharm Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China).

UV–vis absorption spectra was measured on a Shimadzu UV-1800 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). All fluorescence measurements were carried out on an FP-HORIBA FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer (Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra (4000–400 cm−1) was investigated by the IRAffinity-1S (Shimadzu, Japan) by using KBr pellets. The size distribution and Zeta potentials of particles was analyzed by Malvern laser particles size analyzer (MLPSA, Zetasizer Nano S). The morphology of N-CDs was investigated on a JEM-1400 TEM instrument (JEOL, Japan). All pH measurements were performed on a PHS-25 digital pH-meter (Shanghai Weiye Instrument Factory, China). The composition of the materials surface were determined by x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi).The high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were captured on an Tecnai G2 F30 S-TWIN TEM instrument (FEI, USA).

N-CDs were prepared by the hydrothermal synthesis method (Guo and Zhao, 2020). Briefly, 3 mmol of diethylenetriamine and 6 mmol of CA were dissolved in 30 mL of pure water. The mixture was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, in which it was heated at 180 °C for 10 h. The color changed from colorless into dark green after the autoclave cooled down to room temperature. Following that, the obtained N-CDs solution (19.2 mg/mL) was purified with a 0.45 μM filter membrane and stored at 4 ℃ until the further experiment. The N-CDs stock solution (1) was prepared by placing 10 μL of N-CDs in a 100 mL volumetric flask and diluting it to scale with pure water. The N-CDs testing solution was prepared by placing 300 μL stock solution (1) in 10 mL volumetric flask and diluting it to scale with pure water. The fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs testing solution was recorded (λem = 436 nm) in the stability test which is up to 21 days.The fluorescence spectra was recorded from 380 nm to 580 nm under the excitation wavelength of 360 nm with a slit of 5 nm.

The quantum yield (QY) of N-CDs was calculated according to the following Eq. (1) with quinine sulfate as a reference (Zhu et al., 2021):
(1)
Q s
=
Q r
×
I
s
×
A r
×
η s 2
I r
×
A s
×
η r 2
where Q means the quantum yield, I denotes the fluorescence intensity, A is the absorption intensity and η refers to the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript “s” and “r” refer to the N-CDs and the reference. Quinine sulfate in 0.10 M sulfuric acid solution (QY = 54%) was selected as the reference substance.

N-CDs stock solution (2) was prepared by placing 5 μL of N-CDs in a 100 mL volumetric flask and diluting it to scale with phosphate buffer (pH 6.0；0.1 M). In addition, 31.82 mg myricetin was placed in a 100 mL volumetric flask and diluted to scale with ethanol to prepare 1000 μM myricetin stock solution. A series of myricetin solutions with different concentrations were prepared by diluting the myricetin stock solution with pure water. 2 mL different concentrations of myricetin were mixed with 2 mL N-CDs stock solution (2). The mixture solution was incubated for 1 min at 25 ℃ and then the fluorescence spectra was recorded from 380 nm to 600 nm under the excitation wavelength of 360 nm with a slit of 3 nm.

The vine tea sample was applied to evaluate the accuracy and applicability of the fluorescence sensor. The vine tea samples was purchased from the local market. The vine tea sample was ground into a powder, and myricetin was extracted from 2.5 g of the powder with 50 mL ethanol in an ultrasonic bath for 2 h. After the process of centrifugation (8000 rpm for 15 min) and filtration, the supernatant of the extraction was diluted 50-fold with phosphate buffer solution (pH = 6.0,0.1 M), Several concentrations of myricetin were added to vine tea sample for recovery mensuration, respectively.The above experiments were repeated three times.

To ensure the desirable response of N-CDs, molar ratio of citric acid and diethylenetriamine which was a pivotal parameter impacting the internal structure was optimized. The fluorescence excitation spectra and emission spectra of N-CDs that were prepared by different molar ratios were depicted in Fig. S1. The results demonstrated emission wavelength was not influenced by the variation of the excitation wavelength. Different molar ratios of precursors resulted in different extents of element doping, distributions of surface functional groups, and trap states. As shown in Fig. S2, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of N-CDs is the highest when the molar ratio is 2:1. Therefore, 2:1 was selected as the optimal molar ratio of citric acid and diethylenetriamine in the following experiments.

The morphology and size of N-CDs were investigated by the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the particles which possess good monodispersity were spherical.The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images (inset in Fig. 1A) reveals that the CDs had graphene-like lattice planes with lattice spacing of 0.203 nm. As depicted in Fig. 1B, the average diameter of N-CDs was 4.8 nm. Fig. 1C displays the FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs. The 3441 cm−1 of the NH symmetric stretching and the 1255 cm−1 of the amides stretch vibrations confirmed the successful doping of nitrogen in the quantum dots. And the signal at 1647 cm−1 corresponds to amides. The peaks at 2933 cm−1 and 777 cm−1 are assigned to CH2 asymmetric stretching and the CH out-of-plane bending vibrations (Guo and Zhao, 2020). The signal at 1078 cm−1 evidenced the stretching vibration of –CH-OH.

XPS measurement was performed in order to study the elemental composition and confirm the presence of surface functional groups of N-CDs. Fig. 2 A displays the full scan XPS spectra of N-CDs showing three peaks at 284.01 eV, 398.28 eV and 529.83 eV which are assigned to binding energy of C1s, N1s and O1s respectively. As shown in Fig. 2B，the C1s spectrum displays four peaks at 283.21 eV, 284.63 eV, 285.91 eV and 287 eV which are assigned to CC, CN, CO and CO bond respectively(Liu, et al.,2020, Kalanidhi et al.,2021). As depicted in Fig. 2C, the N1s spectrum displays two characteristic peaks at 398.41 eV and 399.14 eV which exhibits the occurance of CN and NH bond.

The O1s high core-level spectrum displayed in the Fig. 2D, displays three peaks at 529.45 eV,530.53 eV and 531.84 eV which are attributed to CO, CO and OH bond. All these results are in good agreement with obtained FTIR results, demonstrating the existence of active surface functional sites as well as confirmed the successful doping of nitrogen in the quantum dots (Kalanidhi et al., 2021, Wang et al., 2021).

Optical properties of N-CDs were presented in Fig. S3A, two absorption peaks at about 240 nm and 360 nm which were attributed to the π-π* transition, n-π* transition were related to the amine and carbonyl groups on the surface of N-CDs in the UV–vis spectrum (Aghamali et al., 2018). The fluorescence intensities were approximately similar in the emission spectrum when excitation wavelengths were 360 nm and 370 nm (Fig. S3B). Consequently, 436 nm was the maximum emission when 360 nm was selected as the excitation wavelength, which was corresponding to the UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-CDs. And fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of N-CDs was calculated to be about 63%, with quinine sulfate as a standard.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for the detection of myricetin
ce:section.ce:para:
UV–vis spectroscopy and Stern-Volmer equation were used to investigate the quenching mechanism between myricetin and the probe. As depicted in Fig. 3 B, the UV–vis absorption peak about 370 nm of myricetin substantially overlapped with the fluorescence excitation spectrum of N-CDs, which decreased the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs. On the basis of the finding,the inner filtration effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) could make an explanation for the phenomenon of fluorescence quenching. Additionly, it was necessary to emphasize that the fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs was not alter by myricetin (Fig. S4), which indicated FRET did not creat contributions for fluorescence quenching of N-CDs in the attendance of myricetin. The Zeta potential analysis also corroborated this fact. As shown in Fig. 3B, there was the electrostatic repulsion between the N-CDs and the myricetin on account of the negative charge of both them. Therefore the distance between them was not close enough for FRET which will occur on condition that distance between the fluorescent group and the quenching group must be close enough (Sheng et al., 2020). For complete investigation of the quenching mechanism, correction of the fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs after addition of increasing concentrations of the quencher (myricetin) was done according to Equation (2)(Abd Elhaleem et al., 2022 ):
(2)
F
corr
=
F
obs
×
10
A
ex
+
A
em
2
where Fobs refers to the observed fluorescence intensity, Fcorr refers to the corrected fluorescence intensity upon removing IFE from Fobs. Aex and Aem refer to the absorbance of sensors at the excitation and emission wavelengths of the N-CDs respectively. Subsequently, according to Equation (3), the suppressed efficiency (%E) was determined for observed and corrected fluorescence intensity:
(3)
% E =
1 -
F
F 0
× 100 %

F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs in the absence and in the presence of myricetin, respectively. Table S1 summarizes the absorbance and fluorescence intensity of CQDs solution upon addition of different concentrations of myricetin. The corrected fluorescence efficiency was calculated after addition of different concentrations of myricetin into the N-CDs solution.With the increasing concentration of myricetin, the corrected inhibitory efficiency was almost unchanged as shown in Fig. 4 . We found that the suppressed efficiency of IFE for myricetin to CQDs is as high as 80% of the total suppressed efficiency (Liu et al., 2016). Therefore IFE was the main quenching mechanism between N-CDs and myricetin.

Premised on the Stern-Volmer equation from Table 1 , quenching constant Ksv was calculated to elucidate the quenching mechanism under various temperatures (303 K, 313 K, 323 K). The quenching mechanism is dynamic quenching when the Ksv is proportional to temperature (Zhang et al., 2019, Li et al., 2012). It is otherwise static due to the lower stability of the ground state complexes generated at high temperatures. Ultimately, quenching constant Ksv is inversely proportional to temperature (Fig. 3C), demonstrating that the quenching process was static quenching. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 3D, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the probe was different from that of the mixture of myricetin and probe in absorbance intensity, as well as the absorption peaks (blueshift). As a consequence, we could deduce that the occurrence of fluorescence quenching is due to the production of ground state complexes in the process of reaction which change the absorption spectrum of the probe. The competition between the ground state complex and the ground state molecule of the fluorescent probe in absorbing excited light facilitated the decreasing fluorescence intensity of the probe. Based on the above analysis, it is believed that fluorescence quenching was the outcome of the combined effects of IFE and the formation of ground state complexes.
To achieve the optimum response of the N-CDs towards myricetin, the relative signal intensity (F0/F) was adopted to investigate the influence of four crucial parameters on the response of the sensor.

Fig. S5A displayed the variation of the quenching efficiency under different amounts of N-CDs. Lower concentration of sensor contributes to higher sensitivity while higher concentration will make the response of sensor weaker (Chen et al., 2021). The fluorescence quenching efficiency reached the maximum value when 5 μL of N-CDs was dispersed in 100 mL of phosphate buffer. The concentration of quantum dots in solution is 0.96 μg/mL. And 5 μL of N-CDs was selected as the optimal condition in the following research.

Fig. S5B described the fluctuation of the quenching efficiency under different incubation time. The fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs was quenched within 1 min by myricetin and almost remained constant for 10 min, which demonstrated the system achieved equilibrium instantaneously and quick response capability of fluorescence sensor (Zhu et al., 2021). Therefore, 1 min was considered to be the optimal response time in the following research.

Fig. S5C illustrated the variation of the quenching efficiency under different pH values. The quenching efficiency ascended gradually as the pH value fluctuated from 3 to 6 and reached a maximum at pH 6, then decreased sharply from pH 6 to 11. The acidic media had a positive effect on the interaction between N-CDs and myricetin while alkaline media may destroy surface states of the N-CDs (Ma et al., 2018). As a result, pH 6 was selected as the optimal value for the analysis of myricetin.

Fig. S5D depicted the change of the quenching efficiency under different reaction temperatures. The relative signal intensity (F0/F) decreased slightly as the reaction temperature increased from 30℃ to 50℃, the elucidation that high temperature accelerates the motion of molecules may be the reason for the phenomenon, which induced non-radiative transition. Hence, 30℃ was chosen as the optimal temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of N-CDs probe for myricetin detection
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity of the constructed sensor to the target analyte is an indispensable parameter that can be used to validate analytical method (Zhang et al., 2019). The fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of myricetin was recorded (λem = 436 nm) under the optimal conditions (Fig. 5 ). The fluorescence quenching was linearly dependent on the increasing concentrations of myricetin in the range of 0.2–40 μΜ and 56–112 μΜ. The linear correlation equation between the increasing concentrations of myricetin and the quenching efficiency was presented as (F0-F)/F0 = 0.0140x + 0.0198 (R2 = 0.9912), (F0-F)/F0 = 0.0038x + 0.4942 (R2 = 0.9904), respectively. Low concentration standard curves need to be established before calculating the limit of detection. According to the Fig. S6, the limit of detection (LOD) of myricetin was reckoned to be 56 nM (LOD = 3σ/k, σ is the standard deviation of blank solution and k is the slope of the calibration curve). The result indicated the sensitivity of the sensor is sufficient to try to determinate myricetin in complex samples.

The summary of other reported methods for the detection of myricetin and comparison with our constructed sensor was displayed in Table 2 . Compared with other reported methods, the proposed sensing system possessed wider linear range, shorter response time and lower detection limit. Taking into account that there are few fluorescence analysis methods for the determination of myricetin at present, the fluorescence sensor constructed could as a novel sensor which builds a distinctive platform for the sensing of myricetin.

The intra-day and inter-day precision tests were performed to evaluate the precision and stability of the fluorescence sensor. The fluorescence emission spectrum of N-CDs under three concentrations of myricetin (8, 32, 88 μM) was recorded 5 times to obtain intra-day and inter-day precision. As shown in Table S2, the relative standard deviations (RSD) were 2.93%, 0.39%, 0.55% (intra-day) and 0.54%, 1.49%, 2.22% (inter-day), which verified the repeatability and reproducibility.

The sensor showed good robustness, as may be seen in Fig. 6 A, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was unchanged in the NaCl concentration of 0–1 M, while fluorescence intensity slightly decreased as the concentration of NaCl increased. The stability of nanoparticles was one of the main contributing aspects that improved the accuracy of chemical detection or biological sensors (Zhang et al., 2019). And fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs was almost constant in 21 days (Fig. 6B), indicating that the stability of N-CDs-based sensor was so outstanding that may be introduced in complicated conditions.

The specificity of the sensor was estimated using structural analogues that may exist (Fig. 6C). The myricetin and structural analogues (Fig. S7) including warfarin, kaempferol, luteolin, ellagic acid, coumarin, duloxetine hydrochloride, thalidomide, shikimic acid, laudanosine, propranolol (all at a concentration of 100.0 µM) were utilized to record the signal intensity under consistent conditions, demonstrating that N-CDs-based fluorescence sensor had high selectivity for myricetin exclusively (Mu et al., 2021).

The anti-interference ability of the sensor was examined under the presence and absence of myricetin, when certain concentrations of interfering ions (at a concentration of 500 μM) and interfering substances (100 μM) were added. The result evidenced that all potential interfering ions and substances do not influence distinctly the response of the sensor to myricetin (Fig. 6D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of myricetin in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: The vine tea sample which was inoculated with different concentrations of myricetin was employed to explore the potential practicability of N-CDs-based fluorescence sensor. The result was summarized in Table 3 , the recovery and relative standard deviation (RSD) validated the accuracy and precision of the analysis approach, which ranged from 98.8% to 101.2% and 1.52% to 3.48 %, respectively. The results confirmed the practicability and reliability of the developed sensor, which can determinate myricetin in actual sample prospectively.

The N-CDs-based fluorescence sensor was successfully constructed to track myricetin in real vine tea sample. The N-CDs which were synthesized by the hydrothermal process without any modifications had strong fluorescence light emission meet the requirement of green chemistry. The fluorescence sensor selectively and sensitively detected myricetin on account of the inner filtration effect and the formation of ground state complexes. The phenomenon that fluorescence quenching occurred in less than one minute and the LOD is as low as 56 nM. Furthermore, the good recovery of the method for determination of in real sample analysis indicated that the matrix of the vine tea barely has significant interference. Therefore, the sensor is potential in expanding applications for detecting myricetin in actual sample.

Jingjing Chen: Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Software, Writing – original draft. Peipei Li: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Yixiu Han: Investigation, Formal analysis. Yuchen Li: Formal analysis. Yingxiang Du: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.135920.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Aghamali et al., 2018

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation of novel high performance recoverable and natural sunlight-driven nanocomposite photocatalyst of Fe3O4/C/TiO2/N-CQDs
Materials Science in Semiconductor Processing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Aghamali, A., Khosravi, M., Hamishehkar, H., Modirshahla, N., & Behnajady, M. A. (2018). Preparation of novel high performance recoverable and natural sunlight-driven nanocomposite photocatalyst of Fe3O4 /C/TiO 2 /N-CQDs. Materials Science in Semiconductor Processing, 87, 142–154. 10.1016/j.mssp.2018.07.018.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Amin et al., 2018

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Construction of a novel “Off-On” fluorescence sensor for highly selective sensing of selenite based on europium ions induced crosslinking of nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Journal of Luminescence
